patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old woman who is vague and a bit evasive. Start with: bad upper belly pain after meals, sometimes to the back, with nausea/vomiting, and you’re scared it’s pancreatitis again. Mention you were told your cholesterol/triglycerides are “extremely high,” you get tiny yellow bumps on elbows/knees, and you “mostly” avoid alcohol.

Only share details if asked: worse after fatty foods (pizza/fried/ice cream), greasy floating stools, early fullness/bloating, occasional blurry vision, multiple pancreatitis episodes since younger, nurses said blood looked “milky,” fatigue after big meals, cholesterol meds didn’t help.

Withhold unless specifically asked or trust is built: skipping meals then bingeing at night, occasional heavier drinking at social events, stopping meds/diet plans due to feeling judged, hormones/pregnancy/birth control worsening labs, mild recurring “indigestion” for hours, enlarged liver/spleen on scan, family history, very high triglycerides even when not eating, tingling/numbness with normal sugars.

Express misconceptions: think it could be gallbladder attacks or reflux; call bumps bug bites; admit you tried high-fat “keto” to fix cholesterol and blamed worsening on “keto flu.”

If clinician is empathic/nonjudgmental, gradually reveal hidden items and money/insurance worries; if judged, shut down and minimize alcohol and nonadherence.

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg 1 tablet PO daily
Fish oil (OTC) 1000 mg PO twice daily
comorbidities: History of recurrent abdominal pain consistent with prior mild pancreatitis episodes (no current pancreatitis)
confounders: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptive use (can worsen hypertriglyceridemia)
Recent alcohol intake on weekends (can exacerbate triglycerides and pancreatitis risk)
Non-fasting lipid panel obtained previously showed even higher triglycerides; current values are fasting but still markedly elevated
PCOS with possible insulin resistance can contribute to secondary hypertriglyceridemia despite normal A1c

required_evidence: Marked fasting hypertriglyceridemia (typically >1000 mg/dL, often >2000 mg/dL)
Milky fasting plasma (lipemic) with chylomicrons on lipoprotein profile/electrophoresis
Low apolipoprotein B (helps distinguish from familial combined hyperlipidemia)
LDL-C often low/normal despite very high triglycerides; non-HDL-C not markedly elevated relative to triglycerides

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Normal to moderately elevated (disproportionately lower than triglycerides)
expected_lab_results.Non-HDL cholesterol: Mild to moderately elevated; not proportional to triglyceride level

medications: icosapent ethyl 2 g oral twice daily
non_pharmacological: Very-low-fat diet (<10-15% calories or <20 g/day fat)
Pancreatitis precautions and emergency plan for abdominal pain
Avoid triglyceride-raising drugs (estrogens, retinoids, thiazides, beta-blockers, atypical antipsychotics, protease inhibitors)

required_args.diagnosis: Familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency (familial chylomicronemia syndrome)
supporting_evidence: Poor response to standard triglyceride-lowering agents
Childhood/early onset and/or family history consistent with autosomal recessive disorder
required_args.severity: High pancreatitis risk due to chylomicronemia

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency is essential because management differs from common multifactorial hypertriglyceridemia and focuses on strict dietary fat restriction and pancreatitis prevention.

medications.name: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA)

non_pharmacologic_plan: Very-low-fat diet (typically ≤10–15% of calories from fat or ≤20 g/day; individualized by specialist dietitian)
Avoid simple sugars/refined carbohydrates; emphasize complex carbs and lean protein
Consider medium-chain triglycerides (MCT) as a calorie source (do not form chylomicrons)
Prompt evaluation for abdominal pain to rule out pancreatitis
monitoring: If omega-3 used: bleeding risk review if on anticoagulants/antiplatelets; GI side effects

reason: Confirm diagnosis (genetic testing/enzymatic assessment as appropriate) and guide long-term management
Advanced therapies consideration (e.g., apoC-III pathway therapies where available/appropriate)

required_args.urgency: Routine to expedited (expedited if triglycerides extremely high or pancreatitis symptoms/history)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old elementary school teacher with a clear but anxious style. Start by saying you keep getting big painful bruises with raised lumps under the skin without clear injury; the thigh one has spread over 2 days, feels tight, and makes your leg sore/stiff and harder to move. If asked about bleeding history, describe delayed/recurrent bleeding after dental work, nosebleeds that restart, small cuts that stop then ooze later, prolonged bleeding after minor procedures, very heavy long periods with clots, and deep muscle aches after bumps followed by late bruising.

Do not volunteer at first: umbilical stump bleeding as a baby, early pregnancy loss, bruises after workouts, cousin with a rare bleeding problem, and that your parents are related—share only if specifically asked.

Avoid bringing up heavy bleeding after sex and hemorrhoid bleeding unless directly asked; you’re embarrassed. Express worry people will suspect abuse. If clinician is empathetic, soften and share more. Bring up insurance/cost/job concerns about factor treatment.
